slides.title: Real time bidding - how advertising interacts with your privacy

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: hi all! welcome

chat.author: Thomas McCarthy-Howe
chat.text: BTW, not seeing slides on the onsite tool?

chat.author: Peter Koch
chat.text: me neither

chat.author: Jessica Batke
chat.text: me either

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Working okay on full tool

chat.author: Frode Kileng
chat.text: https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/126/materials/slides-126-pearg-real-time-bidding-how-advertising-interacts-with-your-privacy-00

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: @Thomas McCarthy-Howe slides in the on-site tool should only be visible if you're on your laptop or tablet, but won't be displayed on the phone

chat.author: Sara Dickinson
chat.text: @meetecho - can you check please

chat.author: Peter Koch
chat.text: tnx, definitely laptop

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: From the phone you need the full client to see slides

chat.author: Jessica Batke
chat.text: i'm on my laptop, slides in every other onsite meetecho have worked the last few days

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: it might be because Johnny is sharing screen and not MeetEcho slides

chat.author: Thomas McCarthy-Howe
chat.text: Confirmed that the full tool works. I had picked the onsite tool

chat.author: Thomas McCarthy-Howe
chat.text: Both on desktop

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: Ah yes, then no, the onsite tool never subscribes to WebRTC media

chat.author: Lorenzo Miniero
chat.text: (which is what screensharing uses)

chat.author: Peter Koch
chat.text: understood, thanks!

chat.author: Thomas McCarthy-Howe
chat.text: :call_me:

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: This is why ad blockers are a thing

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Hopefully a different IAB?

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: not to be confused with the Internet Architecture Board which is a largely-benign

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: or so they say.....

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Interactive_Advertising_Bureau

chat.author: Allison Mankin
chat.text: Of course: IAB - advertising org - not the I*'s IAB here

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: We'll be doing questions at end

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: (feel free to get in queue though ofc)

chat.author: Tom Newton
chat.text: All this data and I still get ads for stuff I bought last week

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: SUbject access  request?

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: Google and Facebook do it on their own platforms

chat.author: Alexandr Railean
chat.text: According to the GDPR, you ought to be able to request the data they have on you, and potentially correct it ;-)

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: do it = show what you're classified as

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: some of the work on Webloc has shown how law enforcement and intelligence agencies are purchasing the data from these data brokers

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: i'm obviously biased :)

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: Side note: "smart" TVs also track and transmit viewing habits, IIRC gathering data as frequently as every 50ms.  This includes content viewed in monitor mode or equivalent.  See paper by Imperial College et al.

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: there are many many websites or consent platforms that don't honor your consent

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: there's many research papers measuring this

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: agree with Nick that persistent ids are especially bad in the server-to-server case, which the browser can't help against. if you enter your real email address, that can be used as a match key

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: I wonder much data is needed from how many sources to effectively de-anonymise the subjects

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: Email Verification Protocol is one place for us to consider that could provide an easy-to-access high-assurance unique identifier

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: I certainly take Johnny's point that probabilistic identifiers (including fingerprinting) should be protected against as well. but persistent cross-context unique IDs are ubiquitous and harmful and keep getting introduced even when some are removed

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: @Nick can you say more about how email verification protocol does that?

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text:
Email Verification Protocol
https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/draft-hardt-email-verification-01
is being proposed for sites to very quickly confirm (with a cryptographic guarantee from the email provider) your email address, without having to send you an email and getting you to click to confirm, like when logging in

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: this talk is terrifying

chat.author: Allison Mankin
chat.text: Yes

chat.author: Tara Tarakiyee
chat.text: What I like about this talk is that it's a reminder that privacy is about economy just as it is about cryptography

chat.author: Shivan Sahib
chat.text: https://arxiv.org/abs/2602.16800

chat.author: Andrew Campling
chat.text: @Chairs: not really a mitigation but why not consider a second edition of RFC 7258 with the focus on the tech industry rather than government?

chat.author: Allison Mankin
chat.text: Or both, Andrew

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: I'm interested in work on private relays

chat.author: Nick Doty
chat.text: I may still not understand how CFR is different or needs to be different from other private relay proposals
